course_description:
This course will involve the study of a topical legal issue or field of law. Topics will vary from year to year. Refer to the Law School website for the current topic.

LAWS5225 offers students a chance to learn about, be part of, and contribute to the world of academic publishing by working as a student editor for the University of Queensland Law Journal (‘UQLJ’). The UQLJ is a generalist, open-access legal periodical which publishes double-blind peer-reviewed scholarly articles by domestic and international authors on a wide variety of legal topics. Publishing since 1948, the UQLJ is one of Australia’s oldest University law journals and is led by a team of Academic Editors.

LAWS5225 offers a limited number of undergraduate law students the opportunity to work as Student Editors with the UQLJ. Under the supervision of the Academic Editors, students will prepare submitted scholarly manuscripts for final publication. This role includes citation-checking, proof-reading and ensuring compliance with the Australian Guide to Legal Citation (4th ed). Students will also submit a case note or book review to demonstrate their own research and writing skills, and appreciation of academic publication standards and processes.

Additional timetable information
The course will involve a compulsory in-person training and induction workshop. After that, coursework will be completed more flexibly, at the instruction of Academic Editors.
course_aims:
Education Mission Statement

The mission of the School of Law is to educate you in and about the law. We seek to assist you to think critically about the law and to investigate its structures, principles, policies, and values. This is achieved by engaging with you as fellow investigators in a range of critical and intellectual conversations about the law and its place in society. Our role is thus to guide you in your own intellectual journey in the law and to equip you with the necessary skills that will enable you to continue that journey long after you have left the School.

Course Aims

LAWS5225 aims to support the publication of high-quality open-access academic articles in the University of Queensland Law Journal. Students will learn, develop and apply high-level academic editing skills independently and collaboratively, supported by the Academic Editors and fellow Student Editors. Students’ will also develop and demonstrate their own research and writing skills through the submission of a book review or case note of a quality appropriate for submission to an academic journal.

learning_outcomes.description: Demonstrate strong proficiency in the editing, proofreading and formatting-for-publication of academic articles.
Apply the Australian Guide to Legal Citation (AGLC) accurately and consistently, in line with the Journal’s internal policies and style guides.

Demonstrate strong appreciation of academic integrity through rigorous citation checking and verification of sources, and alertness to ethical writing and citation practice. Appropriately recognise, reflect upon, and respond to academic integrity issues as they arise.

Recognise, reflect upon, and appropriately respond to ethical issues that arise in the editing process including conflicts of interest, intellectual property and the role of anonymity and confidentiality in academic publishing.

Produce original legal writing of a standard appropriate for public dissemination, demonstrating originality, insight, high-level research and writing skills, and scholarly engagement with contemporary legal issues and/or scholarship.
Learn and work with autonomy, accountability, professionalism and reliability.

Collaborate effectively within an editorial team under faculty editor supervision, developing professional skills in communication, time-management, ethical decision-making, and peer-feedback in a scholarly publishing environment.

assessment_details.title: Book review or Case note
assessment_details.due_date: 22/05/2026 2:00 pm Instructions for this assignment will be discussed in the Induction Workshop. It may be handed in at any point in the Semester (22 May at the latest).
assessment_details.task_description: Each student editor shall be required to: (a) Write and submit a case note on a recent Australian judicial or decision in any area of law that has implications beyond the decision in the case itself; or (b) Write and submit a book review of a recently published legal book in any area of law. Use of AI: This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI and/or MT technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct . To pass this assessment, students will be required to demonstrate detailed comprehension of their written submission independent of AI and MT tools. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
Further details will be provided on Blackboard. The task will be assessed on a pass/fail basis on the basis of the marking criteria (provided via Blackboard).
Deferral or extension
You may be able to apply for an extension .
The maximum extension allowed is 7 days. Extensions are given in multiples of 24 hours.
Extension to Assessment Due Date
The maximum extension for an assessment item due within the semester / teaching period in which the course is offered is 7 days. In exceptional circumstances, an extension may be approved for a further 7 days. Where a student is incapacitated for a period longer than 14 days, they are advised to apply for removal of course.
A student may apply for an extension to assessment due date if they are unable to meet an assessment deadline due to extenuating circumstances. Please refer to the Applying for an extension page on my.UQ for further details on acceptable reasons for an extension and for instructions on how to apply . Additional information on extensions can be found within the Assessment Procedure .
Spoken or written notification of difficulties with assessment deadlines to a course coordinator or the School does not constitute an authorised extension.
A request for an extension to an assessment due date must be submitted through my.UQ as soon as it becomes evident that an extension is needed, but no later than one calendar day after the assessment task submission due date and time . Requests for extensions received after the application due date and time, must include evidence of the reason for the late request, and will require the decision maker listed in the Decision-Maker Tables in the Student Grievance Resolution Procedure to determine whether to accept the request for consideration.
Late submission
A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
This assignment is marked pass/fail. However, a nominal percentage grade will be assigned, such that if the assignment is handed in late, 10% (of the maximum mark) will be deducted for every 24 hours. An assignment which would receive less than 50% will receive a fail grade. For example, if an assignment would have been graded 65/100 and is 3 days late, it will incur a penalty of 30/100, and final grade of 45/100 (a fail overall).

assessment_details.task_description: Each student editor will undertake the following four tasks for their assigned articles over the course of the Semester, in addition to further group and editorial tasks: Ensuring Comprehensive Compliance with the AGLC4 and UQLJ Style Guide Citation and Quotation Checking Formatting - Apply the UQLJ’s house style consistently across the manuscript, including headings, footnotes, tables, case names, legislation, and other typographical elements, and prepare the article for publication. 4. Proofreading - Conduct a thorough line-by-line comprehensive review of finalised manuscripts. Use of AI: This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeks From Week 1 To Week 13 (23 Feb - 31 May)
learning_activities.topic: Induction In the early weeks of the semester, the students will have seminars with the faculty Editors of UQLJ on basic professional training for academic editing, clarification of responsibilities and enhancing writing skills.

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeks From Week 1 To Week 13 (23 Feb - 31 May)
learning_activities.topic: Journal Editing Students will refine their skills in four domains of editing, referencing verification, proofreading and formatting throughout the semester under the direction and guidance of the faculty Editors of UQLJ.
